short_description_of_the_course.label: Short Description of the Course
short_description_of_the_course.value: The main goal of this course is to highlight the intriguing interplay between computational thinking and foundational principles of game theory. We investigate the mathematical concepts that model strategic interactions between different selfish entities, and we use the algorithmic theory lens in order to analyse the existence and quality of equilibrium solutions, study their computational hardness, and design mechanisms to incentivize desirable behaviour. The rigorous study of these notions gives students the fundamental tools to predict and analyse the outcomes in complex scenarios, fostering a deeper understanding of the underlying incentive structures and decision-making processes that play an important role in many modern, real-life applications, where the Internet has been established as the main platform for computing and interaction.

co_requisites.label: Co-requisites
co_requisites.value: Algorithmics II (H) (or equivalent) is recommended but not required

timetable_and_length_and_frequency_of_te.label: Timetable and length and frequency of teaching sessions
timetable_and_length_and_frequency_of_te.value: 3 hours per week, organized as: one 2-hour lecture per week and one 1-hour tutorial per week

course_aims.label: Course Aims
course_aims.value:
Provide students with a good understanding of the foundations of the field of Computational Game Theory (CGT).
Engage them into a critical discussion about some of the key game-theoretic concepts used in modern applications within Computer Science and Economics.
Expose them to the historical and technical interplay between Computer Science, Mathematics, and Economics, as this is realized through the development of CGT.
Give a glimpse into the motivation, toolbox, and the necessary fundamentals of some key research areas within CGT, after which students should feel comfortable to start following the related work on their own.

intended_learning_outcomes_of_course.label: Intended Learning Outcomes of Course
intended_learning_outcomes_of_course.value:
By the end of this course (conditioned on the list of topics chosen) students will be able to:
Compare and evaluate various important equilibrium notions used in game theory analysis; based on that,
Create games that have desirable strategic outcomes.
Evaluate the computational hardness of finding such equilibria.
Familiarize themselves with important results, key mathematical notions, and historical points in the development of (Computational) Game Theory; have a critical understanding of their significance within the field and be able to draw new connections between them.
Evaluate the loss in performance of a system due to selfish behaviour (“Price of Anarchy”) 
Understand the concept of learning dynamics in game-playing, such as best-responses or no-regret learning. Evaluate the performance of such dynamics and use them critically, depending on the application, as an alternative to traditional, static equilibrium concepts.

description_of_summative_assessment.label: Description of Summative Assessment
description_of_summative_assessment.value:
Final examination 70%
Two take-home assessed exercise sets 15%

formative_assessment_feedback.label: Formative Assessment & Feedback
formative_assessment_feedback.value: Verbal feedback on weekly tutorial exercises (given to students in advance) or quizzes. Written feedback on assessed exercise sets.

additional_relevant_information.label: Additional Relevant Information
additional_relevant_information.value:
A list of potential topics, together with a corresponding structural organization of the course are given below.
Introduction
Normal-form games
Basic solution concepts: dominant strategies, pure and mixed Nash equilibria
Mixed Nash Equilibria (MNE)
Quick introduction to Linear Programming duality
Von Neumann’s Minimax Theorem
Existence of MNE in zero-sum games
Existence of MNE in general games: Nash’s Theorem
The Lemke-Howson algorithm
Approximate MNE; the Lipton-Markakis-Mehta algorithm
Pure Nash Equilibria (PNE)
Potential games and existence of PNE
Best-response dynamics
Complexity of finding PNE: easy and hard congestion games
Learning Dynamics
Online learning and no-regret algorithms
Fictitious Play; Karlin’s conjecture
No-Regret learning in zero-sum games
(Coarse) correlated equilibria
Total-search Complexity Classes
From P and NP, to TFNP
Local-search and the complexity class PLS
Fixed-point theorems, Sperner’s Lemma, and the complexity class PPAD; the Daskalakis-Goldberg-Papadimitriou theorem 
Social Choice Theory
Ordinal and cardinal preferences; von Neumann-Morgenstern utilities
Fairness notions
Voting; Arrow’s Impossibility theorem
Cake-cutting
Mechanism Design
(Direct-revelation) mechanisms; truthfulness
Myerson’s Lemma
The VCG mechanism; the Gibbard-Satterthwaite theorem
Revenue-maximizing auctions
Approximately optimal auctions; the Bulow-Klemperer theorem
Prophet inequalities
